my_thoughts: The agreement check works: asking 'So you agree that doing injustice is shameful?' after they've already said suffering is preferable. This is clever—they've already committed to a moral hierarchy, so the logical chain will follow naturally. The student asserted preference (defensive rationalization), which shows they understand: suffering = preferable (moral stance). They'll likely agree to the logical steps next. Hypothesis: students who assert moral preferences follow logical chains more cooperatively than those who resist—testable by tracking whether moral assertions lead to logical compliance.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student asserts preference ('To suffer it. No one would choose the other')—defensive rationalization via pushes back. They understand: suffering injustice = preferable to doing it (moral stance). The comparison_contrast mechanism asks: agreement check ('So you agree that doing injustice is shameful?'), builds: logical chain (shameful → bad → worse). Pattern: moral assertion → agreement check → [next: logical step]. Medium confidence because Socratic method typically leads to cooperative logical following when student has already taken moral stance.
visible_response: So you agree that doing injustice is shameful?
